exp05_timer0_normal.title: Level 5: Timers (Timer0 Normal Mode)
exp05_timer0_normal.aim: To use Timer 0 in Normal Mode and the Overflow Interrupt on the ATmega328P to blink an LED without blocking the main program.
exp05_timer0_normal.objective: Understand how 8-bit timer registers (TCCR0B, TIMSK0) work, calculate prescaler and timer overflows, and use ISR(TIMER0_OVF_vect) for precise timing.
exp05_timer0_normal.theory:
Hardware Timers

Instead of software delay loops (like _delay_ms), the ATmega328P contains dedicated hardware counters called Timers. Timer0 is an 8-bit timer, meaning it repeatedly counts from 0 up to 255. Once it hits 255 and tries to go to 256, it overflows back to 0.

The Prescaler

Running at 16 MHz, a timer would overflow in just 16 microseconds! A Prescaler slows down the clock feeding the timer by dividing it. If we set a prescaler of 1024 (using the TCCR0B register), the timer ticks at 16,000,000 / 1024 = 15,625 Hz. One overflow then takes roughly 16.38 milliseconds.

Timer Interrupts

When the timer overflows, it sets a flag (TOV0). By configuring the TIMSK0 (Timer Interrupt Mask Register) to enable the Timer Overflow Interrupt, the CPU immediately calls the Overflow ISR (Interrupt Service Routine). This frees up your main loop to handle things like reading buttons simultaneously, because the blinking is handled asynchronously in the background.

pretest.question: Which of these registers controls the prescaler for Timer0?
pretest.explanation: TCCR0B (Timer/Counter Control Register 0 B) handles setting the prescaler and starting the timer clock.

pretest.question: If Timer0 is an 8-bit counter, at what value does it naturally overflow when running in Normal Mode?
pretest.explanation: The counter holds 8 bits (0-255). It overflows when trying to step past 255.

pretest.explanation: DDR configures the direction of each GPIO pin.

procedure: Include <avr/io.h> and <avr/interrupt.h>.
Set the DDR for an LED pin (e.g., PB5) connected to Pin 13 to act as an output.
Configure TCCR0B to set the prescaler for Timer0 to 1024 by setting CS02 and CS00 to 1.
Enable the Timer0 Overflow Interrupt by setting TOIE0 to 1 in the TIMSK0 register.
Turn on global interrupts using sei();.
Write the ISR(TIMER0_OVF_vect) routine.
Inside the ISR, setup a static counter variable. Increment it every overflow.
Since one overflow is ~16ms, toggle the LED state when the static counter reaches 61 (1000ms / 16.38ms). Reset the counter to 0.
Leave the main loop (while(1)) completely empty. The background hardware handles everything!

posttest.explanation: The CPU endlessly spins in the empty loop, continuously checking if an interrupt has been flagged by the hardware module.

exp12_spi_master.title: Level 12: SPI Comm (Master Mode)
exp12_spi_master.aim: To execute high-speed bidirectional communication by configuring the ATmega328P as an SPI Master.
exp12_spi_master.objective: Understand the Serial Peripheral Interface (SPI) pins (MOSI, MISO, SCK, SS), configure SPCR (Control Register), and transmit data using SPSR and SPDR.
exp12_spi_master.theory:
SPI Architecture

SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface) is a synchronous, full-duplex protocol. It is vastly faster than UART (up to 8 Megabits per second). It uses 4 dedicated hardware pins:
MOSI: Master Out Slave In.
MISO: Master In Slave Out.
SCK: Serial Clock generated ONLY by the Master.
SS: Slave Select, driven LOW by the Master to tell a specific component on the bus to listen.

Full Duplex Exchanging

In SPI, reading and writing happen simultaneously. As the master shifts an 8-bit command bit-by-bit out of MOSI, the slave device shifts an 8-bit response bit-by-bit into MISO via shift registers. To read a byte from a slave, you MUST transmit a dummy byte (like 0xFF) just to generate the 8 clock pulses on SCK to suck the answer in.

Configuring the SPCR

The Master MUST explicitly set the MOSI, SCK, and SS pins as OUTPUTs in DDRB. The MISO pin must be an INPUT. The SPI Control Register (SPCR) enables SPI (SPE=1) and explicitly asserts Master mode (MSTR=1).

pretest.question: Why must the ATmega328P (as Master) explicitly set the SS (Slave Select) pin to OUTPUT and keep it HIGH if it’s not communicating, even if it uses another pin to select the slave?
options: If SS is an input and pulled LOW externally, the hardware violently forces the ATmega328P into Slave Mode, destroying Master operations
To tell the UART hardware to turn off
Because the SPCR register requires it for SPI clock division
pretest.explanation: This is a critical hardware feature. If the physical SS pin (PB2) is configured as input and drops to LOW, it is an over-ride that disables the MSTR bit in SPCR.

procedure: Configure DDRB explicitly. Set MOSI (PB3), SCK (PB5), and SS (PB2) as outputs. Keep MISO (PB4) as an input.
Drive SS High (PORTB |= (1<<2)) so no slaves are falsely selected yet.
Initialize the SPI Control Register (SPCR). Enable SPI (SPE=1), Master (MSTR=1), and optionally set the clock rate to fosc/16 (SPR0=1).
To transmit, pull the target slave's chip select pin LOW.
Load data into the SPI Data Register: `SPDR = data;`
Wait for the SPI Interrupt Flag (SPIF) in the SPSR register to be set to 1.
The received answer from the slave is now living inside `SPDR`. Read it, then pull the chip select pin HIGH to end the transaction.

exp09_adc_polling.title: Level 9: Analog-to-Digital Conversion
exp09_adc_polling.aim: To understand how the Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) built into the ATmega328P transforms continuous physical voltages into discrete numeric values.
exp09_adc_polling.objective: Understand the ADC Multiplexer Selection register (ADMUX), the ADC Control/Status register (ADCSRA), and how to read the 10-bit result in ADCH/ADCL via software polling.
exp09_adc_polling.theory:
The Real World is Analog

Light levels, temperatures, and audio signals don't exist merely as 0V or 5V (digital); they are continuous voltages anywhere in between. Microcontrollers only understand digital code. The Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) bridge is this gap.

10-Bit Resolution

The ATmega328P's ADC has 10 bits of resolution. It slices the 0V to 5V input range into 1024 unique steps (2^10). At 5V reference, each step is roughly 4.88mV. An input of 0V reads as 0, 2.5V reads as 511, and 5V reads as 1023.

Connecting to the ADC Multiplexer

The ATmega328P has only ONE physical ADC circuit, but it can read from multiple pins (A0-A5) using an internal switch called a Multiplexer (MUX). We configure the ADMUX register to tell the ADC which pin to connect to. We also configure the ADCSRA register to turn the ADC on (ADEN) and set the ADC clock speed using its dedicated prescaler (ADPS0-2).

Triggering a Conversion

Once configured, writing a 1 to the ADSC (Start Conversion) bit in ADCSRA asks the ADC to take a reading. Converting takes time (13-260 microseconds). We use a while loop to wait (poll) until the hardware clears the ADSC bit to 0, which means the conversion finished and the result is waiting in the ADC data registers (ADCL and ADCH).

pretest.explanation: To save space and power, there is only one physical ADC that rapidly 'switches' (multiplexes) between the different analog input pins when requested.

exp15_watchdog_timer.title: Level 15: System Stability (Watchdog Timer)
exp15_watchdog_timer.aim: To use the Watchdog Timer (WDT) to automatically reset a crashed or deadlocked microcontroller.
exp15_watchdog_timer.objective: Understand how the separate on-chip Watchdog oscillator functions, configure WDTCSR for various timeout lengths, and execute the wdt_reset() macro.
exp15_watchdog_timer.theory:
What is a Watchdog Timer?

In the real world, software can crash. A robot driving a car will crash if an infinite loop holds up the main processing. A Watchdog Timer (WDT) is a completely separate hardware timer (running on its own internal 128kHz oscillator) whose sole purpose is to count up. If it ever reaches its specified timeout value, it violently forces a hard reset on the entire ATmega328P.

Feeding the Dog

The only way to prevent a reset is for the main software to routinely 'feed' or 'kick' the dog by sending it a reset signal. In C, we do this by calling the wdt_reset() function inside our main loop. If our main loop freezes unexpectedly, wdt_reset() is never called. The watchdog keeps ticking, eventually hitting its timeout and generating a System Reset.

Watchdog Reset Flag (WDRF)

When the system restarts, you might want to know WHY it restarted. The MCUSR (MCU Status Register) contains a WDRF flag. If WDRF is 1 immediately after booting, you know a watchdog reset occurred!

options: The Watchdog Timer resets the microcontroller completely when the timeout period elapses
The CPU runs at double the clock speed
pretest.explanation: The Watchdog cannot be stopped by software bugs. If it is not 'fed', it will inevitably force a hardware reset.

procedure: Include <avr/wdt.h> at the top of the program.
Wire an LED to Pin 13 to serve as an indicator.
In setup, blink the LED rapidly 5 times to prove the ATmega328P has booted up.
Enable the Watchdog Timer with a 2-second timeout using wdt_enable(WDTO_2S);.

Now intentionally freeze the program with an empty infinite loop: while(1);.
Observe the LED. 2 seconds after the empty loop begins, the chip will forcefully reset, and you will see the 5 rapid boot-up blinks again.

exp10_uart_tx.title: Level 10: Serial Comm (UART Transmit)
exp10_uart_tx.aim: To configure the hardware Universal Synchronous/Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter (USART) to transmit plain text serial data over a USB-Serial connection.
exp10_uart_tx.objective: Understand asynchronous communication basics like baud rate (UBRR0), framing format (UCSR0C), and how to check the Data Register Empty flag (UDRE0) before transmitting raw chars.
exp10_uart_tx.theory:
Serial vs Parallel

Instead of using 8 wires to send a byte (like writing to PORTD), serial communication sends bits down a single wire one after another. UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter) is standard for this. It is termed 'Asynchronous' because there is no clock wire shared between the two devices; they must both independently agree beforehand on exactly how fast the bits will arrive.

The Baud Rate

This agreed-upon speed is the baud rate. Common speeds are 9600 or 115200 bits per second. The ATmega328P uses the 16-bit UBRR0 register to divide its 16MHz clock down into the baud rate. The formula is: UBRR0 = (F_CPU / (16 * Baud)) - 1. For 9600 baud at 16MHz, UBRR0 is 103.

Framing and UDR0

When you place an 8-bit character into the UDR0 (USART Data Register), the hardware UART takes over. It adds a START bit, outputs the 8 data bits sequentially over the TX pin (PD1), and adds a STOP bit (the Frame). Because this takes time, you CANNOT write a second character to UDR0 immediately. You must wait (poll) the UDRE0 (USART Data Register Empty) flag in UCSR0A. Only when it is 1 is the register empty and ready for the next character.

pretest.explanation: Because there is no hardware clock to sync them, mismatched baud rates lead to incorrect framing logic and data corruption.

exp01_led_blinking.title: Level 1: Hello World (LED Blinking)
exp01_led_blinking.aim: To write an Embedded C program to blink an LED connected to an ATmega328P.
exp01_led_blinking.objective: Understand GPIO configuration using DDRx and PORTx registers, and learn how to stall the MCU using delay routines.
exp01_led_blinking.theory:
What is GPIO?

General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) pins allow microcontrollers to read digital signals (like buttons) or write digital signals (like turning on an LED).

The Registers

  • DDRx (Data Direction Register): Configures a pin as INPUT (0) or OUTPUT (1). For example, setting DDRB = 0xFF makes all PORTB pins outputs.
  • PORTx (Data Register): When a pin is configured as an output, writing a 1 to PORTx sets the pin HIGH (5V), and writing a 0 sets it LOW (0V).

The Delay Loop

Microcontrollers operate infinitely fast (up to 16 million instructions per second on an Arduino UNO). If you turn an LED ON and OFF without a delay, it happens so fast that it will just look dimly lit. A delay function forces the microcontroller to count empty cycles, keeping the LED in its current state long enough for the human eye to see.

exp08_pwm_phase_correct.title: Level 8: PWM (Phase Correct)
exp08_pwm_phase_correct.aim: To use Timer 1 in Phase Correct PWM mode to generate symmetric, noise-free pulses suitable for motor control and servos.
exp08_pwm_phase_correct.objective: Understand how dual-slope Phase Correct PWM differs from Fast PWM, and configure ICR1 or OCR1A to define custom PWM frequencies.
exp08_pwm_phase_correct.theory:
Dual-Slope Counting

Unlike Fast PWM which counts up to TOP and immediately restarts at 0 (single-slope), Phase Correct PWM counts up from 0 to TOP, and then counts back down from TOP to 0 (dual-slope). This creates a symmetrical pulse centered exactly half-way through the PWM cycle, which is vastly preferred in motor control to avoid sudden phase shifts and current spikes.

Defining Custom TOP with ICR1

Normally a 16-bit timer counts to 65535, giving a very slow PWM frequency. By using Phase Correct PWM mode with ICR1 (Input Capture Register) defining the TOP value, we can create custom-frequency PWM! For example, standard RC Servo motors expect a 50Hz signal (a 20ms period). We calculate what ICR1 needs to be so the timer reaches TOP in exactly 10ms (half the 20ms dual-slope cycle).

Controlling Servos

A servo expects a 50Hz signal where the HIGH pulse lasts between 1.0ms (0 degrees) and 2.0ms (180 degrees). We use OCR1A to define how long the pulse stays high within that 20ms window.

exp13_i2c_master.title: Level 13: I2C Comm (TWI Master)
exp13_i2c_master.aim: To address multiple devices over the I2C (Two-Wire Interface - TWI) bus using only two synchronous pins: SCL and SDA.
exp13_i2c_master.objective: Understand the TWI Control mechanisms: TWCR, TWSR, and TWBR, generate a START/STOP condition, and address unique 7-bit slave devices.
exp13_i2c_master.theory:
The I2C Bus Architecture

Unlike UART (point-to-point) or SPI (multi-CS), I2C uses Open-Drain circuitry and unique addressing. Only SDA (Serial Data) and SCL (Serial Clock) are needed to connect up to 127 unique devices to the ATmega328P. Both lines must be pulled up to VCC with external resistors.

Master Control: TWCR

The TWI Control Register (TWCR) manages every single bus action. A transaction is initiated by generating a START condition: writing `(1<

Addressing Slaves

After a successful START, the master transmits the 7-bit Slave Address appended with a Read/Write bit (0 for Write, 1 for Read) out of TWDR. The designated slave responds by pulling SDA Low during the 9th clock cycle, known as the Acknowledge (ACK).

procedure: Initialize the bus speed (SCL Frequency) using the TWI Bit Rate Register (TWBR).
Enable the TWI hardware explicitly (TWEN = 1) in TWCR.
Formally generate a START condition on the bus. (TWCR = (1<<TWINT)|(1<<TWSTA)|(1<<TWEN)).
Wait for the hardware to confirm it successfully grabbed the bus by checking when the TWINT bit sets to 1: `while (!(TWCR & (1<<TWINT)));`.

Clear TWINT and enable TWI again to send the address: `TWCR = (1<<TWINT) | (1<<TWEN);`.
Wait again. After ACK, load real data into TWDR. Clear TWINT again.
After all data is sent, generate a STOP condition: `TWCR = (1<<TWINT)|(1<<TWEN)|(1<<TWSTO);`.

exp04_external_interrupts.title: Level 4: External Interrupts (INT0 & INT1)
exp04_external_interrupts.aim: To use external hardware interrupts on the ATmega328P to pause the main program and execute an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR).
exp04_external_interrupts.objective: Understand how interrupt vectors function in C (using ISR macros and AVR/interrupt.h), configure EICRA and EIMSK, and debounce interrupts in software.
exp04_external_interrupts.theory:
What is an Interrupt?

An interrupt is an emergency signal from hardware that tells the CPU to immediately stop what it's doing (the main loop) and jump to a specific piece of code called an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR).

Configuring Interrupts

In the ATmega328P, INT0 (Pin 2) and INT1 (Pin 3) are the primary external interrupt pins.

  1. EICRA (External Interrupt Control Register A): Configures exactly what triggers the interrupt (e.g., rising edge, falling edge, low level, or any logic change).
  2. EIMSK (External Interrupt Mask Register): Actually enables the specific interrupt pin you want to use (INT0 or INT1).
  3. sei(): The global interrupt enable macro. Even if you turn on the INT0 pin, nothing will happen until you execute sei(); to tell the CPU to accept all interrupts.

The Global Variable gotcha

Whenever a variable is written in an ISR and read in the main loop, it MUST be declared with the volatile keyword (e.g., volatile uint8_t flag = 0;). This tells the C compiler that the value can change without warning, preventing it from optimizing the variable out of existence.

exp03_seven_segment.title: Level 3: Driving a 7-Segment Display
exp03_seven_segment.aim: To write a program to display the digits 0-9 sequentially on a common-cathode 7-segment display.
exp03_seven_segment.objective: Understand how to map digital outputs using a lookup table (array) and drive multiple GPIO pins in parallel using PORT registers.
exp03_seven_segment.theory:
What is a 7-Segment Display?

A 7-segment display is an array of 7 individual LEDs (segments a, b, c, d, e, f, g) housed in a single package. By illuminating a specific combination of these LEDs, you can display any decimal digit from 0 to 9.

Pins and Parallel Outputs

Rather than turning on each segment individually in code (which is tedious and slow), we configure an entire 8-bit PORT (like PORTD) as an output with DDRD = 0xFF;. Then, by assigning an 8-bit hex or binary value directly to PORTD, we can change all 8 pins at exactly the same time. The order in which we wire the microcontroller to the 7-segment display must match the binary mapping we use in our software.

Lookup Tables

A lookup table is a powerful and very fast technique in C. We simply define an array (e.g., const uint8_t digits[10] = {0x3F, 0x06...};). The index of the array directly maps to the digit we want to display, making sequential counters incredibly simple to write with a for loop.

exp02_push_button.title: Level 2: Reading Inputs (Push Button)
exp02_push_button.aim: To write an Embedded C program to read the state of a push button and control an LED accordingly on the ATmega328P.
exp02_push_button.objective: Understand GPIO input configuration using DDRx, PORTx, and PINx registers, including internal pull-up resistors and debouncing techniques.
exp02_push_button.theory:
Configuring Inputs

When you want to read a digital signal on a GPIO pin, you configure it as an INPUT. In the ATmega328P, setting a bit in the DDRx register to 0 configures the corresponding pin as an input.

The PINx Register

To actually read the voltage state on an input pin (whether it is 5V or 0V), you must read the PINx (Port Input Pins) register. For example, if PD2 is set as an input, reading PIND & (1 << 2) will tell you if the pin is HIGH or LOW.

Internal Pull-up Resistors

A bare push button connected between a pin and Ground causes a problem when the button is NOT pressed: the pin is 'floating', and its voltage is unpredictable. To fix this, you enable the microcontroller's internal pull-up resistor. When the pin is an INPUT (DDRx is 0), writing a 1 to the corresponding bit in the PORTx register connects an internal 20k-50kΩ resistor to 5V (VCC). The pin will naturally read HIGH when unpressed, and LOW when pressed (connected to Ground).

Debouncing

Mechanical switches 'bounce' when pressed, meaning the metal contacts hit each other multiple times before settling. In software, this looks like multiple rapid button presses. A simple wait (delay) after the first state change helps ignore these bounces (software debouncing).

pretest.question: Which register is used to READ the current logical state of an input pin on the ATmega328P?
pretest.explanation: The PINx register contains the actual physical logic levels present on the pins.

Set DDRD bit 2 to 1, then clear PORTD bit 2 to 0
pretest.explanation: To enable the pull-up, set the direction to input (DDR bit = 0) and the port value to high (PORT bit = 1).

exp07_pwm_fast.title: Level 7: Pulse Width Modulation (Fast PWM)
exp07_pwm_fast.aim: To use Timer 0 in Fast PWM mode on the ATmega328P to fade an LED in and out (analog-like output).
exp07_pwm_fast.objective: Understand how to configure TCCR0A and TCCR0B to enable Fast PWM on OC0A (Pin 6/PD6), altering the duty cycle via OCR0A.
exp07_pwm_fast.theory:
What is PWM?

Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) is a technique for getting analog results with digital means. Digital control is used to create a square wave, a signal switched between on and off. By rapidly turning the output on and off, the average voltage delivered to a component (like an LED) changes, giving the illusion of fading.

Duty Cycle

The duty cycle is the percentage of time a digital signal is ON over an interval or period of time. A 50% duty cycle means the signal is HIGH 50% of the time and LOW 50% of the time, resulting in an average voltage of 2.5V (half of 5V).

Configuring Fast PWM

We use the dedicated hardware output compare pins (e.g., OC0A on PD6) driven by Timer0. We configure the Waveform Generation Mode bits in TCCR0A/B to Fast PWM mode. In this mode, the timer counts from 0 to 255 repeatedly. When TCNT0 matches OCR0A, the OC0A pin resets to LOW. When TCNT0 overflows back to 0, the OC0A pin sets HIGH. Changing OCR0A changes when the pin drops LOW, modifying the duty cycle.

exp14_eeprom_rw.title: Level 14: Non-Volatile Memory (EEPROM)
exp14_eeprom_rw.aim: To durably store and retrieve configuration variables on the ATmega328P through power cycles using internal EEPROM.
exp14_eeprom_rw.objective: Understand the EEPROM Address (EEAR), Data (EEDR), and Control (EECR) registers, and safely observe hardware wait-states for erase/write cycles.
exp14_eeprom_rw.theory:
What is EEPROM?

Most memory (SRAM) is erased instantly when the power turns off. The ATmega328P contains 1024 bytes (1 KB) of Electrically Erasable Programmable Read-Only Memory (EEPROM), which retains data completely without power.

Writing to EEPROM

Writing to EEPROM is slow (3.3ms) because it physically alters trapped electrons. When the power is lost during a write, EEPROM corrupts. To write a byte:
1. Wait until any previous write is finished (poll EEPE inside EECR).
2. Load the physical memory address (0-1023) into EEAR.
3. Load the 8-bit data into EEDR.
4. Temporarily disable interrupts using `cli()`.
5. Set the Master Write Enable bit (EEMPE) in EECR.
6. Within exactly 4 clock cycles, set the Write Enable bit (EEPE).

Reading from EEPROM

Reading is extremely fast compared to writing. You wait until write operations are complete, supply the address into EEAR, set the Read Enable bit (EERE), and then copy the resulting byte pulled into EEDR.

pretest.question: Why is it mandatory to run 'cli()' (Clear Interrupts) before triggering the Master Write Enable (EEMPE) bit?
options: To avoid corrupting the UART hardware
Because the final EEPE write must occur within exactly 4 clock cycles, and an ISR triggering in between would cause the write to be aborted
To speed up the EEPROM oscillator
To clear the previous data from the registers
pretest.explanation: The 4-cycle timeout is a strict hardware security measure against accidental erratic writes when voltage drops.

exp06_timer1_ctc.title: Level 6: Timers (Timer1 CTC Mode)
exp06_timer1_ctc.aim: To use Timer 1 in Clear Timer on Compare Match (CTC) mode to generate precise, hardware-driven signal timings.
exp06_timer1_ctc.objective: Understand how 16-bit timers operate using TCCR1A, TCCR1B, OCR1A, and TIMSK1. Calculate the exact Match values for OCR1A to trigger a 1Hz interrupt.
exp06_timer1_ctc.theory:
CTC Mode: Auto-Resetting Timers

In Normal mode, the timer counts up to its maximum value (255 or 65535) and overflows. What if we want it to reset earlier to get an exact 1-second delay instead of ~16ms? CTC mode solves this! The timer continually compares its count (TCNT1) against our requested value in OCR1A. If they match, the timer automatically zeroes itself and triggers an interrupt.

Timer1 at 16-bits

Timer1 is composed of two 8-bit registers (High and Low) that merge to count to 65,535. This allows for massive, incredibly slow counting. At a 16MHz clock with a 1024 prescaler, it ticks at 15,625 Hz.

Finding the OCR1A Value

To get a 1-second interval, we need 15,625 ticks. Since the timer starts at 0, our count needs to be 15,625 - 1 = 15,624. So, we simply set OCR1A = 15624;.

exp11_uart_rx_interrupts.title: Level 11: Serial Comm (UART Receive Interrupts)
exp11_uart_rx_interrupts.aim: To construct an interrupt-driven UART receiver that automatically catches characters arriving via Serial without polling.
exp11_uart_rx_interrupts.objective: Understand the RXCIE0 interrupt, safely buffer incoming characters inside the ISR(USART_RX_vect), and securely pass them to the main loop.
exp11_uart_rx_interrupts.theory:
The Problem with Polling Rx

Unlike transmitting (Tx), where you decide when to send, receiving (Rx) is totally unpredictable. A user could type a character at any microsecond. If you use a blocking `while(!(UCSR0A & (1<

The RX Complete Interrupt

By enabling the Receiver Interrupt Enable (RXCIE0) bit in UCSR0B, the UART hardware will automatically trigger an interrupt the instant a new byte completely finishes shifting into the ATmega328P. You write an ISR(USART_RX_vect) that reads the UDR0 register and saves the character to a `volatile` buffer (like an array or ring buffer).

Interrupt Handlers

When the ISR fires, global interrupts are momentarily disabled. You must read UDR0 inside the ISR immediately, or the next incoming character might overwrite it before your main loop processes it.

Enable BOTH the Receiver (RXEN0) and the Transceiver (TXEN0) in UCSR0B.
Enable the Receiver Interrupt (RXCIE0) in UCSR0B.
Declare a `volatile unsigned char last_rx_char;` and a `volatile uint8_t char_ready;` flag globally.

Write ISR(USART_RX_vect). Inside, read the incoming byte from UDR0 into `last_rx_char`, and set `char_ready = 1;`.
In your main loop, continuously check if `char_ready` is 1.
When `char_ready` is 1, clear it to 0, and transmit the received character back out so you can see it on the monitor (this is called Echoing).
